metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten test assertion (b) to require the exit-10 path specifically: assert the process/return-code from the losing clone's `claim_compute` call is `ConcurrencyMismatch` (exit 10) with the 'claim lost on cross-clone merge' message — remove the 'or re-read' fallback.
findings.finding: The 'or' in test assertion (b) — '(the exit-10 path or, on re-read, `assignee != self`)' — means the two-clone regression test can pass without the exit-10 path in claim.py ever firing, leaving the primary new production behavior unexercised by any AC.
findings.location: Step 4 / AC: 'New two-clone concurrent-claim regression test' — assertion (b)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace assertion (b) with: 'the losing clone's claim_compute returns a ConcurrencyMismatch signal (exit 10) with message containing "claim lost on cross-clone merge"', and drop the fallback `assignee != self` branch from the test assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add docs/concurrency.md to the What/files section and state the minimum content required (e.g., 'add a subsection describing exit-10 on claim loss, STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED fsck finding, and show annotation'), so the AC is objectively evaluable.
findings.finding: AC4 ('docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour') has no corresponding step in the What/files section, leaving the executor without guidance on what must be written and no way to verify the criterion is met beyond a subjective read.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; 'What / files' section (absent entry)
findings.suggested_fix: In What/files, add: '**docs/concurrency.md** — add a "Cross-clone claim surfacing" subsection describing: (a) exit-10 on claim loss, (b) STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED fsck finding with example output, (c) show annotation for affected tickets.' Then update AC4 to reference those three named items.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan how the post-push re-read handles the case where `push_after_commit` silently fails (no remote, network error, or merge-conflict exhaustion) — the plan currently asserts the local store will reflect 'the merged store' but `push_after_commit` is documented as best-effort and may return without merging remote state, causing the ownership check to falsely report a win.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that after `push.push_after_commit(tracker)` the clone can 're-read the ticket from the merged store' to determine the authoritative assignee — but `push_after_commit` is documented as 'Best-effort auto-push ... never raises' (push.py L233-253) and may silently return without performing any merge (e.g. no remote configured, network failure, merge-conflict exhaustion). In those cases, re-reading the local store post-push yields only THIS clone's own EDIT event for assignee, not a cross-clone merged result, making the ownership check falsely report 'you won' when no remote merge ever occurred.
findings.location: Plan § What/files item 1 (Post-claim ownership verification); src/rebar/_commands/claim.py L173-176; src/rebar/_store/push.py L233-253
findings.suggested_fix: The plan should either (a) specify a fallback detection path (e.g. 'if push returned without merging, the re-read is skipped and a PUSH_PENDING_CLAIM_UNVERIFIED warning is emitted rather than a false win'), or (b) document that the exit-10 check only fires when the push+merge round-trip succeeded (gating the re-read on whether the local HEAD advanced past the pre-push HEAD).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the mechanism for the post-push re-read: either (a) document that it only fires when push succeeded with merge-as-union and specify a fallback for async/offline modes (e.g., deferred detection at next `show`/`list` read), or (b) replace the unconditional re-read with a conditional check — only re-read and compare `assignee` when `push_after_commit` can confirm it ran a merge (e.g. by returning a bool flag), and document that async/off modes defer ownership detection to the read path.
findings.finding: The post-push `assignee` re-read check fires on the LOCAL store state, which may not yet reflect the merged remote state when `push_after_commit` does not complete the merge-as-union — specifically when the push is configured async (`sync.push=async`), when the remote is unreachable (all 3 retries exhausted), or when `push_after_commit` is a no-op (`sync.push=off`). In all three cases, the re-read will see the local clone's own EDIT(assignee) event and return the claimant's own name — the loser incorrectly concludes it won. The plan states the check happens 'after `push.push_after_commit(tracker)`' but `push_after_commit` is unconditionally best-effort and returns `None` regardless of whether a merge happened (`push.py` line 250: `except Exception: logger.warning(...)`). The correctness of the ownership check is therefore contingent on a successful merge-as-union having run — an ordering assumption the plan does not acknowledge.
findings.location: What / files — Item 1 (Post-claim ownership verification); AC line: 'A claim that loses the cross-clone race is reported to the loser via the exit-10 path once the merge is visible locally'
findings.suggested_fix: Return a boolean from `push_after_commit` (or a separate fetch+reduce helper) indicating whether a merge-as-union was actually applied; only invoke the ownership check when that flag is True. For async/off modes, document that `assignee != self` detection is deferred to the next read-path command (show/list) rather than guaranteed at claim time. Update AC1 to reflect this conditional guarantee.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in §1 the fallback behaviour when `push_after_commit` is a no-op or the post-push re-read fails: e.g. skip loser-detection with a warning (informational, not exit-10) when no remote is configured, and wrap the re-read in a try/except that degrades gracefully rather than propagating a raw exception.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for the post-push re-read in `claim_compute` (§1) when `push_after_commit` is a no-op (no remote configured) or the subsequent re-read of the merged store fails — leaving the loser-detection logic silently skipped or crashing in common single-remote-less dev setups.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py` (the actual `show` command implementation) as an edit target in the plan's scope, and clarify whether 'annotate' means a new display-side rendering step in `_cmd_show` or relies on `status_fork_resolutions` flowing through `public_state` automatically.
findings.finding: The plan names '`show` annotates the affected ticket from the same derived field' but does not identify a concrete file path. There is no `_commands/show.py`; the show command is implemented in `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py` (`_cmd_show` → `show_state`). If 'annotate' means rendering extra output (e.g. a warning line or an extra JSON key) beyond what `public_state` already passes through, `reads_cli.py` is an unenumerated edit target.
findings.location: Plan section 3 ('show annotates the affected ticket'); src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py (unenumerated)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact `status_fork_resolutions` list-item schema (field names, types, and whether the key is always present vs. absent when empty) in §2, and ensure §3 and §4 reference the same field names — reconcile `loser_uuid` (existing variable) vs. `dropped_uuid` (plan prose) explicitly.
findings.finding: The `status_fork_resolutions` derived-state schema (item field names, list-vs-absent-when-empty, append-vs-replace semantics) is under-specified: §2 names fields `winner_uuid`/`dropped_uuid`/`loser_env_id`, but the existing processor variables (lines 147/157 of `_processors.py`) use `winner_uuid`/`loser_uuid` — the rename to `dropped_uuid` is implicit. §3 (fsck consumer) and §4 (test assertion) both depend on exact field names, and neither specifies whether a ticket with no forks gets an empty list or no key at all.
findings.location: What/files §2 (reducer derived state) + §3 (fsck consumer) + §4 (test assertion c)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test assertion (or a separate single-clone smoke test) that the winning clone's post-merge re-read yields `assignee == self` and exits 0, ensuring the new post-push re-read code path does not produce false-positive exit-10 on the happy path.
findings.finding: The test plan asserts the loser detects exit-10 but does NOT assert that the winning clone's post-push re-read returns cleanly (assignee==self → exit 0). A regression in the new re-read code path could produce false-positive exit-10s for every claim, including the winning clone, yet the test would still pass because it only checks the loser's behavior.
findings.location: AC line: 'New two-clone concurrent-claim regression test: deterministic converged assignee + loser detects loss + fork surfaced' / What §4
findings.suggested_fix: In the two-clone regression test, after both push/merge cycles, assert for the WINNING clone that `claim_compute` (or the re-read result) returns success (exit 0) and `assignee == winning_clone_id`. Alternatively, add a single-clone test in `test_store_concurrency.py` that calls `claim_compute` end-to-end and asserts exit 0 + `assignee == self` to guard the re-read path against regressions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/concurrency.md` to the Scope 'In' list so the required docs update (AC item 4) is consistently captured in both the scope and the acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The Scope section omits docs/concurrency.md from the explicit 'In' list, but AC item 4 requires updating it. A developer following the Scope section would not know docs work is required, creating a gap between the stated scope and the acceptance criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope line, append `, docs/concurrency.md (surfacing behaviour note)` after `tests`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a work item in 'What / files' for updating `docs/concurrency.md` to describe the exit-10 claim-loss signal and the new `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` fsck finding, so AC bullet 4 has a corresponding described deliverable.
findings.finding: AC bullet 4 ('docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour') is an orphan acceptance criterion: it requires a deliverable (documentation update) that appears nowhere in the 'What / files' work-item list (items 1–4) or the Scope section.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 4: 'docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour'
findings.suggested_fix: Add as item 5 under 'What / files': '**docs/concurrency.md** — add a section describing (a) post-claim ownership verification and the exit-10 signal for cross-clone race losers, and (b) the `status_fork_resolutions` derived field and the `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` fsck finding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `'status_fork_resolved'` to `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` in `fsck.py` so the JSON output path emits a structured `{kind, ticket_id, detail}` object for `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` findings (consistent with all other ticket-associated finding kinds).
findings.finding: `fsck.py`'s `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` set is an unenumerated in-file consumer: the new `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` tag must be added to `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` (fsck.py:34-41) for the JSON output path (`_transform_json`) to emit a structured `{kind, ticket_id, detail}` object rather than a flat `{kind, detail}` blob — the same pattern every other ticket-associated finding uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `compact.py` as an edit target and add `status_fork_resolutions` to its compiled_state strip list (alongside `updated_at`) since it is a replay-derived field that must be recomputed fresh, not persisted in the SNAPSHOT.
findings.finding: `compact.py` explicitly strips `updated_at` from `compiled_state` before writing a SNAPSHOT (compact.py:167: `compiled_state = {k: v for k, v in compiled_state.items() if k != 'updated_at'}`). The plan says `status_fork_resolutions` is 'recomputed deterministically on every replay' — implying it is a derived field that should NOT be persisted in SNAPSHOT `compiled_state`. However, the plan does not name `compact.py` as an edit target and does not instruct adding `status_fork_resolutions` to the strip list. If it is included in the SNAPSHOT's `compiled_state` it will be restored by `process_snapshot` and then accumulated again by subsequent replay, potentially creating duplicate entries.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/compact.py:167; plan section 2 ('pure derived ticket state … idempotent on replay')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"status_fork_resolved"` to `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` in `fsck.py` so the JSON-mode parser extracts `ticket_id` from `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` tag lines, matching the structured shape of all other fsck findings.
findings.finding: The new `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` fsck tag is not registered in `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` (fsck.py:34-40), so the JSON-mode parser (fsck.py:486) will NOT extract `ticket_id` from the tag line — it falls into the `else` branch emitting only `{kind, detail}`, silently dropping ticket-id from JSON output.
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` line 34-40, add `"status_fork_resolved"` to the `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `loser_env_id` in Step 2: state the default as the `env_id` (or equivalent clone-identity field) already present in the losing event's envelope, so the executor has a concrete source without guessing.
findings.finding: `loser_env_id` appears in the proposed derived-state schema (`{winner_uuid, dropped_uuid, loser_env_id}`) without defining what it means or where it comes from, leaving the executor to invent the identity source.
findings.location: Step 2 — 'record the resolution into the ticket's DERIVED STATE (e.g. a `status_fork_resolutions: [{winner_uuid, dropped_uuid, loser_env_id}]`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `loser_env_id` with `loser_event_env_id` and note: 'populated from the dropped event's existing `env_id` envelope field — no new identity infrastructure needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'reducer must remain a pure replay function; all event surfacing via derived ticket state consumed by read-side commands' constraint in CLAUDE.md or an ADR (in addition to the user-facing docs/concurrency.md update).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — the reducer records fork resolutions only into derived ticket state (pure function, no I/O, no cross-subsystem imports) as the sole mechanism for surfacing concurrency events — but this constraint is only documented in `docs/concurrency.md` (user-facing surfacing behaviour), not in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc. Future reducer contributors could inadvertently reintroduce side-effects without knowing this invariant.
findings.location: What/files §2 (reducer pure derived state) + Scope ('no side-effecting write from the reducer') + AC bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet: 'CLAUDE.md (or docs/adr/NNN-pure-reducer-derived-state.md) records the architectural invariant: reducers must not perform I/O or cross-subsystem calls; fork-resolution events are surfaced exclusively via derived state fields consumed by read commands.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the post-push re-read in `claim_compute`: if the re-read raises, surface a distinct warning (e.g. 'claim ownership unverifiable after merge — treat as tentative') rather than silently proceeding as if the claim succeeded.
findings.finding: The post-push re-read in `claim_compute` has no specified error handling: if the re-read of the merged store throws (I/O error, corrupt state, lock contention), the loser proceeds silently as if they won — the exit-10 signal is never emitted and the failure is swallowed.
findings.location: What/files §1 — `src/rebar/_commands/claim.py` post-push re-read block; AC line 'A claim that loses the cross-clone race is reported to the loser via the exit-10 path'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the re-read+compare block in a try/except; on failure emit a warning-level message ('could not verify claim ownership post-merge; re-run `rebar show <ticket>` to confirm') and return a distinct signal (e.g. exit 11 or ConcurrencyUnverifiable) so callers can distinguish 'lost' from 'unknown'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/cleanup path for `status_fork_resolutions` (e.g. bounded list, deduplication by uuid pair, or pruning once the ticket is closed) and specify that fsck/show consumers must tolerate the field being absent (default to empty list).
findings.finding: `status_fork_resolutions` on the reduced ticket dict has no specified retirement/cleanup path and grows unboundedly on every replay that encounters a fork; consumers (fsck, show) are not specified to be tolerant of the field's absence on older or unaffected tickets.
findings.location: What/files §2 — `reducer/_processors.py` derived-state record; §3 — fsck/show read-side surfacing; AC line 'Resolved STATUS forks are recorded in PURE derived ticket state'
findings.suggested_fix: In the reducer, use `ticket.get('status_fork_resolutions', [])` when appending; cap the list at a reasonable bound (e.g. 20 entries, deduped by winner_uuid+dropped_uuid). In fsck/show, access the field with `.get('status_fork_resolutions', [])` and skip silently when empty.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exit-10 message for the edge case where the resolved `assignee` is empty/None (e.g. 'claim lost — ticket is now unassigned; re-run claim to retry') and add at least one sentence of recovery guidance to the error output.
findings.finding: The exit-10 UX path does not specify what the user sees or can do when the winning `assignee` value is empty/None after merge (e.g. the ticket was re-opened or the assignee field was cleared in a concurrent event), producing a confusing 'claim lost — now assigned to None' message.
findings.location: What/files §1 — exit-10 message 'claim lost on cross-clone merge (now assigned to <winner>)'; AC line 'A claim that loses the cross-clone race is reported to the loser via the exit-10 path'
findings.suggested_fix: In the exit-10 branch: if winner is falsy, emit 'claim lost on cross-clone merge — ticket is now unassigned. Run `rebar claim <ticket>` to retry.' Otherwise emit the existing 'now assigned to <winner>' message.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for why immediate post-push claim-time detection is preferred over deferred read-side detection (e.g. 'faster feedback to the agent before it starts work') and document the acknowledged limitation that the signal is conditional on a synchronous successful push.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing immediate post-push detection (at claim time) over deferred read-side detection (at the next show/list command). A structurally different alternative — detecting `assignee != configured_claimant` on the read path (e.g. in `show` or `list`) — avoids the ordering/async dependency entirely and is simpler to implement correctly; it would also benefit agents that are offline at claim time and reconnect later. The plan's chosen approach only improves over deferred detection in the case where push succeeds synchronously, but that case is not called out as the design target.
findings.location: What / files — Item 1; Why section; AC line 1

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
